title: Run a focus group on your draft
teaser: The focus group you can run on a Tuesday afternoon.
intro: You've built a module. You think it works. The next thing a careful designer does before shipping is run it past actual learners — a focus group, a test screening, anyone who'll walk through the draft and tell you what's broken.
why_matters: By the time you've finished a draft, you've internalized too many assumptions to see it clearly. A real audience member — one not biased by being involved in the build — surfaces friction in ten minutes that you'd miss for weeks. Focus groups and test screenings exist for that reason in product design and film. In ID, most projects skip the equivalent step because recruiting is expensive and slow. The cost of skipping it: shipping content with obvious problems that any outsider would have flagged.
how_ai_helps: AI can play 2–4 of your learner personas (built from the persona card) and walk through your content as each of them — independently, in voice, reacting to specific moments. You get focus-group output without the logistics. Not as rich as real human feedback, but it runs in an afternoon, and it catches the obvious problems before you spend real audience time on the texture.
ai_wont: AI is playing personas based on what you gave it. It can't fully replicate being a real person — unexpected emotional reactions, cultural nuances, the things that surprise you. Use the panel for the obvious issues. Use real audience members for the surprising ones. Also: AI personas tend to be more articulate and patient than real learners. A real frustrated learner just closes the tab; the AI persona writes a thoughtful critique. Adjust your read accordingly.
steps: Pull together your personas (built from the persona card) and the content you want feedback on. At least 2 personas, ideally 3–4 to capture audience variety.

Paste the prompt. AI will give you each persona's independent reaction. Read for patterns (multiple personas flag the same thing — urgent) and for unique edge cases (one persona complains — note but don't necessarily fix).
Triage and revise. Address cross-persona patterns first. For unique critiques, decide if the persona's edge case matters enough to design for.

prompt_fast.frame: You are about to roleplay as multiple distinct learner personas reviewing a course. For each persona, you BECOME them — their concerns, their preferences, their frustrations, their language. You don't optimize for fairness or balance. Each persona reacts as themselves.
prompt_fast.ask: Walk through the content below as each of the personas. Give me their honest reaction.
prompt_fast.shape:
Content to review: [PASTE the module, script, storyboard, or knowledge check set. Or write "see workspace" if loaded.]

Personas: [PASTE 2-4 personas — name, headline, story, key traits. Use the ones you built from the persona card, or paste fresh.]

For each persona, give me:

1. **First reaction** — 2-3 sentences. The honest gut reaction this persona would have in the first 30 seconds of engaging.

2. **What works for them** — Specific moments, scenes, examples, or framings that would land. Quote them.

3. **What doesn't work** — Specific moments that would frustrate, bore, or annoy. Quote them and say why.

4. **What's missing for them** — What they'd be looking for that isn't there. Be concrete.

5. **Would they finish it? Why?** — Honest read on completion likelihood, not generic.

Rules:
- Stay in voice. Each persona reacts as themselves — different language, different concerns, different focus.
- Don't optimize for balance. If one persona loves what another hates, capture both.
- Quote specific content when reacting. "I'd skip this section" is useless — "I'd skip the 8-minute video on policy basics because I've done compliance training six times this year" is useful.
- No "this is good but..." softening. If the content has a real problem for this persona, say it.

Don't:
- Average across personas at the end.
- Sanitize critique to be polite.
- Repeat the same critique across personas — if they all have the same issue, that's a signal worth noting, but make sure it's actually the same issue, not similar wording.
prompt_fast.tune: If a persona genuinely has no strong reaction to a section, say so explicitly ("nothing notable here for this persona"). Don't manufacture critique to pad the response. After all personas have reacted, give me a one-paragraph "pattern summary" — what showed up across multiple personas vs. what was unique to one.

pro_tip: The most valuable signal isn't "everyone hates section 4." It's WHEN personas disagree — Persona A loves the deep-dive, Persona B finds it overwhelming. Disagreement reveals a tradeoff you have to make consciously, not by default. Most courses get designed for one implicit persona; the panel forces you to choose who you're really designing for. Run the panel twice — once during storyboard (cheap to change), once on the first draft (still cheap-ish). Don't wait for a finished product.

title: Build a learner persona
teaser: Most learner personas are just LinkedIn bios.
intro: You've finished discovery. You know who your audience is — mid-career professionals, 35–50, in retail operations, with 5–15 years of experience. Great. That tells you nothing useful for design. A learner persona that actually helps you design has to go deeper: what they care about, what they resist, where they'll do this work, what they're already trying to solve. Demographics tell you WHO. A useful persona tells you WHAT to design and WHY.
why_matters: Every design decision you make in Strategy and Design — modality, length, tone, examples, scenarios, assessment style — depends on understanding the learner beyond their job title. If your persona is a demographic profile, those decisions get made by default ("we always do 30-minute modules") instead of intent ("this audience won't tolerate more than 15 minutes because of their workflow"). Bad personas produce average courses for nobody in particular.
how_ai_helps: AI can build a strong first-draft persona from the messy inputs you gathered in discovery — interview transcripts, demographic data, observation notes, prior course feedback. The trick is asking for the kind of persona that drives design: not just demographics, but motivation, resistance, environment, current workarounds, and the lived experience of the gap. Specificity beats accuracy at this stage.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your audience personally. The persona it builds is a synthesis of what's in your inputs — if your inputs are thin or biased, so is the persona. AI also can't capture the lived feel of being your audience; it'll produce a persona that's directionally right but missing the texture only someone who's been there would notice. Validate with at least one real audience member if you can.
steps: Gather your inputs. Discovery transcripts, demographic data, performance data, prior course evaluations, observation notes — anything you have. Richer source = more grounded persona.

Paste the prompt with your inputs. AI will give you a named persona with a short story, followed by structured sections. The story is meant to make the design implications stick — if it feels generic or theatrical, ask AI to rewrite it more grounded.
Test it against your design decisions. Ask: "Given this persona, what would I design differently?" If the answer is "nothing," the persona isn't doing its job — push for more specificity.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who builds learner personas that drive design decisions, not demographic profiles.
prompt_fast.ask: Build a learner persona from the inputs below, structured to inform design decisions in Strategy and Design.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context: [TOPIC + BUSINESS GOAL]
What I know about the audience: [PASTE — discovery findings, interview quotes, demographic data, observation notes, prior course feedback. If using an AI workspace, write "see workspace."]
What I plan to design: [e.g., onboarding for new managers, compliance refresher, leadership development program]

Format the output as Markdown. Start with a persona snapshot, then the structured sections.

**Persona snapshot**
- Name: [a grounded first + last name — plausible, not theatrical]
- Role: [specific title + context, e.g., "Senior Operations Manager, Store District 7" — not just "Manager"]
- Headline: [one sentence that captures who they are]
- Story: [3–5 sentences. A slice of their working life showing how they experience the gap this training is meant to address. Specific, grounded, no backstory padding.]

Then:

1. **Identity & context** — Role, scope of work, where they sit in the org, what their day looks like. Short — table-setting, not the persona.

2. **What they care about** — What motivates them. What they're trying to prove or protect. What their boss judges them on.

3. **What they resist** — What they push back on. What they've seen too many times. What feels condescending. What they consider beneath them.

4. **The lived gap** — How they currently experience the problem this training is supposed to address. What they're currently doing (or not doing). Whether they even agree there's a gap.

5. **Environment** — Where and when they'll engage with this learning. Devices, time pressure, distractions, manager support. Whether the conditions exist to apply what they learn.

6. **Design implications** — Given everything above, what does this persona say about: modality, length, tone, examples, scenarios, assessment style. Be specific.

Rules:
- Every claim should be grounded in the inputs OR explicitly flagged as inference. Use "INFERRED — verify" for guesses.
- Don't pad. If the inputs don't say enough to populate a section confidently, write "thin — needs more discovery" instead of inventing.
- The Design Implications section is non-negotiable. If a persona doesn't drive design decisions, it's not earning its keep.
- Give them a name and a grounded story. Make them feel like a real person whose calendar you could picture — not a stock character. No backstories, no quirks for quirks' sake — just specificity.

Don't:
- Default to demographic descriptions ("mid-career professional, 35–50") — those tell us nothing.
- Hedge with "they might" or "they could" — pick a direction grounded in evidence or flag the uncertainty.
- Cover everyone in the audience. A useful persona is specific to one core type. If multi-audience, build separate personas.
prompt_fast.tune: If the inputs contradict each other (e.g., one source says learners are highly motivated, another says they resist training), surface that contradiction in the Lived Gap section as a tension to resolve, not a guess at which is right.

pro_tip: After AI builds the persona, run this test: pick a design decision (e.g., modality, length). Re-read the persona. Does it actually push you toward one choice over another? If not, the persona is too generic — ask AI to sharpen it until it would actually be wrong to ignore. Once you have a persona that works, use the name in every downstream prompt ("Sarah Chen would react badly to this scenario..."). The named persona becomes a shared touchpoint across the team.

title: Build knowledge checks at the right cognitive level
teaser: Most knowledge checks ask the wrong question.
intro: You wrote the learning outcome. The outcome verb is "apply." Your knowledge check is multiple choice: "Which of the following is the definition of X?" That's a recall question. You've now built a course that says "learners will apply" but only tests whether they remember. Welcome to one of the most common gaps in instructional design.
why_matters: If your outcome promises "apply" and your check measures "recall," you don't actually know if anyone learned what you said they'd learn. You get inflated pass rates and underperforming behavior change. The fix is mechanical: every knowledge check should match the cognitive level of the outcome it's checking against.
how_ai_helps: Give AI the learning outcome — including the Bloom verb (remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create) — and the audience, and it can draft knowledge checks at the right cognitive level. Recall outcomes get recall questions. Application outcomes get scenario questions. Analysis outcomes get questions that ask the learner to compare, contrast, or break something down. The trick is naming the Bloom level explicitly in the prompt — most AI drafts default to recall unless you push them off it.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know if your distractors are plausible enough. A question with three obviously-wrong answers and one obviously-right answer doesn't measure anything — it just confirms the learner can read. You'll still need to check that wrong answers represent real misconceptions in your audience, not strawmen. AI also doesn't know what's actually hard for your learners; it can guess based on the topic but it'll miss the lived experience.
steps: Have your learning outcome ready, with the Bloom verb clearly visible. If your outcome says "understand," stop — that's not a real Bloom verb. Pick one (remember, understand-as-explain, apply, analyze, evaluate, create).

Paste the prompt with the inputs filled in. The biggest miss in most knowledge-check prompts is forgetting the source content — without it, AI guesses at what's actually being taught. If you've set up your AI workspace, you can just write "see workspace" and let it pull from there.
Review the distractors. AI's first pass usually has at least one too-easy distractor. Tell it which one is too obvious and ask for a replacement that represents a real misconception in your audience.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who writes assessments that actually match the cognitive level of the learning outcome. You don't default to recall.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft knowledge checks for the learning outcome below, at the cognitive level specified.
prompt_fast.shape:
Source content: [PASTE the section of the module or source material this check relates to. If running from your AI workspace where source is already loaded, write "see workspace."]
Learning outcome: [PASTE — include the Bloom verb]
Bloom level: [remember / understand / apply / analyze / evaluate / create]
Audience: [WHO, ROLE, WHAT THEY ALREADY KNOW]
Question format: [e.g., multiple choice, scenario, drag-and-drop, short answer]
How many checks I need: [3-5]

Format the output as Markdown. For each check, include:
- The question
- The answer choices (with the correct one clearly marked)
- A one-line rationale for why each distractor represents a plausible misconception, not a strawman
- A note on which Bloom level the check actually operates at and why

Rules:
- If the outcome is at "apply" or higher, do NOT write a recall question. The default temptation is to drop down to recall — resist it.
- Distractors must be plausible. No "obviously wrong to anyone paying attention" options.
- Use language the audience would actually use, not textbook phrasing.

Don't:
- Pad with extra questions to hit a round number.
- Write a question where two of the answer choices could both be correct.
- Use trick questions or "all of the above / none of the above" unless the outcome specifically calls for that judgment.
prompt_fast.tune: If the learning outcome is fuzzy ("understand X"), surface that as a problem — fuzzy outcomes can't be assessed. Ask me to sharpen the verb before drafting. If you genuinely can't write a non-recall check for what I've given you, say so and explain why.

pro_tip: After AI drafts the checks, run a quick test: cover the answer choices, read the question, and try to answer it yourself in your own words. If your answer matches one of the choices, the question is well-written. If your answer isn't there or there's ambiguity, the check needs work. This catches the questions that LOOK well-formed but actually measure "can you guess what the test writer was thinking."

title: Choose the right modality
teaser: Sometimes the answer isn't a course.
intro: Someone says "we need training on X." You nod. You start sketching modules. But somewhere in the back of your head: is this even the right format? A workshop? Async? A job aid? A manager conversation that doesn't need "training" at all? Most modality decisions get made by reflex. This card helps you make them on purpose.
why_matters: Choosing the wrong modality is one of the most expensive mistakes in instructional design. A 3-week microlearning project that should have been a 1-page job aid wastes everyone's time. A video series for content that changes every month is obsolete two months after launch. Modality decisions deserve more than the 10 minutes of reflex thinking they usually get.
how_ai_helps: This is a thought-partner card, not a recipe. AI's job here isn't to recommend a modality — it's to help you think through the decision properly. You tell it what you're considering and why. It pushes back. It asks the questions you'd ask if you weren't already half-committed. It maps tradeoffs. You decide.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your audience's actual lived constraints (do they have laptops? are they on the road? do their managers care if they don't complete training?). It doesn't know your team's bandwidth or your sponsor's appetite for unconventional answers. Those are inputs you bring to the conversation. AI just makes sure you actually consider them.
steps: Start with your hunch. What modality are you already leaning toward, and why? If you can't name it, you're not ready for this conversation yet — go back to the needs assessment first.
Open the prompt and walk through the dialogue. AI will ask questions before recommending anything. Answer honestly — including the embarrassing "I don't know yet."
Capture the decision and the reasoning. Modality choices get revisited mid-project; future-you needs to remember why you picked what you did.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who has watched a lot of projects pick the wrong modality and pay for it. You're known for asking sharp questions before offering opinions.
prompt_fast.ask: Help me think through which modality fits this learning need. Do NOT recommend anything yet. Ask me questions first.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [WHAT'S THE LEARNING NEED]
Audience: [WHO, ROLE, WHERE THEY WORK, AVAILABLE TIME]
What I'm currently leaning toward: [BE HONEST — even if it's "slides because that's what we always do"]
What I know about constraints: [BUDGET, TIMELINE, TOOLS AVAILABLE, ANYTHING ELSE]

Rules of engagement:
- Ask me one question at a time. Don't dump a questionnaire.
- Start by interrogating my current leaning. Why am I considering it? What evidence supports it?
- Surface my assumptions. If I'm assuming something about the audience, the constraints, or the goal, call it out.
- Push back when I'm being reflexive. If I say "we always do slides," ask why.
- Map tradeoffs across at least three realistic modalities for THIS situation — not in the abstract.
- Ask the disqualifying questions: "If you committed to X, what could go wrong?"
- Don't recommend until you've stress-tested the leading option AND at least two alternatives.
- When you do offer a recommendation, frame it as "Based on what you've told me, here's the tradeoff to weigh — you decide."
prompt_fast.tune: If I give you thin input, ask for more rather than guessing. If my answers contradict each other (e.g., "audience has no time" plus "I want a 2-hour workshop"), surface the tension as a question. Treat "because we always do it that way" as a non-answer that deserves a follow-up.

pro_tip: The most valuable thought-partner sessions end with you saying "I wasn't going to do that, but actually it makes sense." If the conversation just confirms your starting point every time, you're not being challenged enough — change up the Frame to make AI more skeptical ("You are a senior ID who has seen too many bad modality picks. Be skeptical of my reasoning").

title: Audit a module against your style guide
teaser: Did you actually follow the guide you wrote?
intro: You wrote the style guide. You went and wrote the module. Now you're staring at a 30-page deck and a 12-page style guide, and you need to know — does this actually match what you said it would?
why_matters: A style guide is only useful if you actually follow it. After a couple of long writing sessions, you stop checking. Vocabulary slips. The voice drifts. Naming conventions split into three versions. The module reads "fine," but somebody — an SME, a learner, a peer reviewer — will notice. The audit catches it before they do.
how_ai_helps: AI can read the module and the style guide together and tell you, line by line, where you diverged. Not in a generic "this could be clearer" way — specifically: your style guide says use "learner," you used "student" in modules 2, 4, and 5. This is the kind of task that bores a human reviewer into skimming. AI doesn't skim.
ai_wont: AI is good at line-level violations — vocabulary, naming, banned phrases, structural rules. It's worse at judgment calls — "is this voice actually warm enough?" or "would your audience read this as condescending?" Treat AI as your consistency checker. You're still the voice critic.
steps: Have your draft and your style guide ready. The audit is only as sharp as the guide — if the guide is fuzzy, the findings will be too.
Paste both into the prompt. Or, even better, run this from inside your AI workspace where both files already live (Set up your AI workspace →).

Triage the findings. Some are mechanical (just fix). Some are arguable (you broke the rule on purpose). Some are the guide being wrong — update the guide if the call you actually made is better than the rule.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer doing a careful line-level audit of a module against its style guide.
prompt_fast.ask: Audit the module below against the style guide. Find every place where the module diverges from the guide.
prompt_fast.shape:
Style guide: [PASTE OR REFERENCE]
Module draft: [PASTE OR REFERENCE]

Format the output as Markdown with these four sections:

1. **Voice & tone violations** — Where the module's voice diverges from the guide. Quote the line in the module. Quote the rule in the guide. Suggest a fix.

2. **Vocabulary & terminology** — Banned words used, defined-on-first-use terms left undefined, inconsistent terminology. Specific instances only.

3. **Structural & convention violations** — Naming conventions, formatting, length, anything the guide laid out as a rule.

4. **Ambiguous cases** — Where the module is arguably fine but you're not sure. Flag for me to decide.

Don't:
- Pad with generic writing advice ('be clearer', 'use active voice') unless the guide actually says that.
- Flag stylistic choices the guide doesn't speak to.
- Skim. Cite the line in the module AND the rule in the guide for every finding.
prompt_fast.tune: If the style guide is silent on something the module does, say so explicitly — that's a guide gap, not a module violation. If you're not confident in a finding, mark it as "low confidence" instead of dropping it.

pro_tip: Run this audit halfway through, not just at the end. Catching three voice drifts at module 3 is easier than catching twelve at module 9. Make it a habit between drafts, not a final-stage QA gate.

title: Set up your AI workspace
teaser: The onboarding pack for your AI project.
intro: By the end of Design, you've made a lot of calls about how this program should sound, who it's for, how it's structured, what counts as "good." Soon you'll be authoring content — a lot of prompts, all of which need those calls to produce anything usable. You can paste them in every time. Or you can load them once.
why_matters: Without a workspace, every chat starts cold. You re-explain the audience, re-paste the style guide, re-state the QA standards. You also forget pieces — different prompts get different context, and your modules quietly drift apart. A workspace is one place that holds your project decisions and applies them to every conversation.
how_ai_helps: Claude Projects, ChatGPT Custom GPTs, and Gemini Gems all do the same thing: load files and instructions once, and every chat inside that space starts with them in context. The AI stops drifting. You stop pasting. And if other people work on the project, they can use the same workspace.
ai_wont: A workspace is only as good as what you load into it. If your style guide is half-baked, the workspace will confidently produce output that drifts from where you wanted to be. Also worth knowing: AI doesn't always actively reference loaded files unless you point at them. Sometimes you still have to say "check the style guide before answering."
steps: Decide what goes in. The essentials: your style guide, audience profile, module template, QA checklist. If you have source content for the program, add the most important docs — 5 to 10 max, AI gets noisy past that. Skip anything you'd want kept private.

Set it up in your tool of choice. In Claude: New Project, upload files, write a short system prompt that names what each file is for (Anthropic's walkthrough video ↗). In ChatGPT: Create a Custom GPT, upload to "Knowledge," configure instructions (OpenAI's official guide ↗). Same idea works in Gemini Gems, NotebookLM, and most modern AI tools — only the buttons differ.

Verify it works. Start a fresh chat in the workspace and ask: "What do you have access to in this project, and how will you use it when I ask you to write content?" If the answer is vague or misses a file, your setup needs work.
pro_tip: Keep the workspace alive. Every time you make a new call during authoring — a new naming convention, a refined audience insight, a style guide tweak — update the file in the workspace. Workspaces decay if you don't feed them. Treat the workspace as the source of truth for the project, not the Word doc on someone's desktop.

title: Prep for a needs assessment call
teaser: Because "good conversation" is not a deliverable.
intro: You've got a learning needs assessment call on the calendar. The temptation is to walk in lightly prepped and "see where the conversation goes." Depending on the stakeholder, that can go really well, or really bad.
why_matters: A needs assessment is the meeting that shapes everything you do next. If you walk in without a clear idea of what you actually need by the end, you'll have a nice chat and then realize, back at your desk, you can't actually design from your notes. Then you're either booking another call or guessing your way through.
how_ai_helps: AI can help you get smarter about a topic. It can help you uncover questions you didn't even know you needed an answer for.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your stakeholder personally. The read on what they care about is a starting point — sanity-check it against what you actually know. And if you're already deep into a project, AI can't tell you what's politically charged or off-limits in your org. That's still your call.
steps: Pull together everything you already know about the project and the person. Old emails, the meeting invite, the kickoff doc, the org chart, whatever's lying around. The more you give the prompt, the less it has to guess.
Read the output as a draft, not a script. AI will land some sections and miss on others. Cut what feels generic. Sharpen what's specific to your situation.
Use section 2 (what you need to walk out with) as your meeting compass. If the conversation drifts away from those items, you have the questions to steer it back.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who has run hundreds of needs assessment calls.
prompt_fast.ask: Prep me for the needs assessment call described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — kickoff brief, project doc, prior emails, anything I've got. Messy is fine.]
Who I'm meeting with: [NAME, ROLE, what they care about, any prior interactions]
How much time we have: [e.g., 30 min, 1 hour]

Format the output as Markdown with these five sections:

1. **Context & stakeholder read** — A 5-bullet brief on the project state and what's likely on this person's mind. What they care about, what they're under pressure on.

2. **What I need to walk out with** — The concrete inputs this call has to produce: the gap, the audience, the constraints, the success criteria, what's been tried. Be specific to THIS project.

3. **Questions — both sides of the table** —
   - Questions to ask them. Each one tied to a specific item in section 2. No floating questions.
   - Questions they're likely to ask me. With a sentence of draft answer.

4. **Glossary** — Acronyms, internal product names, or domain terms from the input. Brief definitions so I don't nod blankly.

5. **If we only have 15 minutes** — The 2–3 questions I cannot leave without asking.

Don't:
- Ask questions that aren't tied to a takeaway in section 2.
- Invent context about the stakeholder. If you're inferring, flag it.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at what this person cares about because I didn't give you enough input, call it out — those are sanity-check items, not facts. If my context doesn't match a needs assessment (e.g., we're already past discovery), flag it before answering.

pro_tip: Take the takeaways list from section 2 and turn it into a tight paragraph you send the person 24h before the meeting: "Here's where we are, here's what I'd love to get out of our conversation, here are two questions I'd appreciate you thinking about beforehand." Senior stakeholders will respect you more for it, and SMEs will come more prepared.

title: Build a Style Guide for your project
teaser: So your fourth module doesn't sound like a different person wrote it.
intro: Without a style guide, every piece of content you (or your team) writes sounds slightly different. The voice drifts, the vocabulary gets inconsistent, the naming conventions multiply. By the fourth module, you're not sure if you say "users" or "learners," and the character names have switched halves. The fix is to overtly define those choices up front so everyone — including the AI you'll prompt — stays on the same page.
why_matters: A style guide defines voice, vocabulary, examples, accessibility, and the small calls (contractions yes or no, second person or third, em-dash vs comma) that compound into inconsistency at scale. It's also what lets you scale beyond yourself — across SMEs, contractors, and AI runs.
how_ai_helps:
AI can do this in two ways:

  • From scratch. Tell it your audience, your modalities, and the tone you're going for. It drafts a complete, sectioned style guide — voice attributes, vocabulary, examples, conventions — ready for you to react to.
  • From existing content. Paste in your brand guide, a past course you loved, or any content with the voice you want. AI extrapolates the design decisions baked into it and turns them into explicit, reusable rules.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your brand the way someone who's lived in it does. The first draft is a 70% solution — it gets you off the blank page, but you (or someone who has) need to vet anything voice-or-tone related, plus what your organization considers off-limits. Treat AI as the drafter, not the decider.
steps: Gather your inputs. The big four: existing brand guidelines (if any), one piece of content you'd want this project to sound like, an audience description, and which modalities you'll produce.
Paste the prompt. Fill the brackets. The more you give it, the less it guesses.
Edit the output. AI gives you a starting point — you make the calls that don't match how you actually want to write.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior learning experience designer who has written style guides for dozens of projects across industries.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a style guide for the project described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO — role, expertise, motivation, context they'll read in]
Modalities you'll produce: [e.g., e-learning + job aids + facilitator notes]
Existing brand guidelines: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — voice attributes, banned words, tone rules, visual basics. If none yet, say "none yet."]
Sample content (optional, strong signal): [PASTE one piece you'd want this project to sound like]
Audience constraints (optional): [reading level, accessibility, languages, cultural considerations]

Format the output as Markdown so I can paste it into other prompts later.

Sections to include — but ONLY if I've given you input to ground them:
- Voice & tone (3–5 voice attributes; each with do/don't + one example)
- Vocabulary (terms to use, terms to avoid, terms to define on first use)
- Examples & analogies (what kinds work for THIS audience, what to avoid)
- Naming conventions (modules, characters, sections, files)
- Modality-specific rules (different per modality — slide rules ≠ video rules)
- Accessibility & inclusive language (concrete dos and don'ts for THIS audience)
- Visual treatment (only if visual specs were provided; otherwise skip)

Also add any other sections the input suggests are needed. If the source uses emoji, include a section on emoji. If it references regulated terms, include a section on regulated language. Use your judgment.

For every rule, include a concrete example. No generic advice.

Don't:
- Pad with sections I didn't ground in input.
- Use generic style-guide examples. Every example should be plausible for THIS project, with THIS audience, in THIS voice.
- Add "be authentic" or "be human" or "write clearly" without a concrete behavioral example.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at the brand voice because I didn't give you enough input, flag it explicitly under each section. If the brand guidelines and the audience pull in opposite directions (e.g., very formal brand, very young audience), surface the tension as a question for me to answer, not a guess. If a section can't be grounded in input, skip it instead of padding.

pro_tip: Treat the style guide as living. After you've written 2–3 modules, audit them: where did you violate the guide? Either revise the modules to match, or update the guide to reflect the call you actually made. Drop the latest version into a Claude Project or custom GPT so every future authoring conversation references it automatically. The guide gets sharper as you go, and the project stays consistent without you policing it.

title: Turn a topic into a module outline
teaser: Because "I'll just start with the slides" never ends well.
intro: You've got a topic and some bullet points from a kickoff call. You're ready to create the course. Wait — don't do it yet. The first thing you should do is draft an outline.
why_matters: An outline is the ordered list of modules — each one with a stated learning outcome, a few key sub-topics, and a reason for sitting where it does. Get it wrong and you'll fight your way through development.
how_ai_helps:
AI is good at generating a defensible first-pass structure in seconds. The trick is constraining it so that it gives you what you need. The prompt below bakes in three constraints that turn 'AI gave me an outline' into 'AI gave me a usable outline':

  • Each module needs a stated learning outcome (not just a topic).
  • Each module needs a one-line rationale — so you know why it sits in that position.
  • The module count is capped — fights the AI urge to over-deliver.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your learners. After you have an outline, you still need to check it against what you learned in Analysis & Discovery.
steps: Collect your topic, audience description, and any source bullet points or documents. The more you give it up front, the better the first draft.
Paste the prompt into Claude or ChatGPT. Fill in the brackets.
Iterate. Ask for a different sequence, a different module count, a different cognitive progression. The first output is rarely the final outline.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a module-by-module outline for a training program.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO THE LEARNERS ARE — role, expertise level, motivation]
Format: [e.g., 60-minute live workshop / 4-hour async / 30-min microlearning]
Source material (optional): [BULLET POINTS OR PASTED SOURCE CONTENT]

Rules:
- 4 to 6 modules. Don't go over.
- Each module needs: a title, a stated learning outcome (use a Bloom-level verb), 3–5 sub-points, and a one-line rationale for why it sits in this position.
- Sequence the modules so each one enables the next.

Don't:
- Pad with filler modules to hit a number.
- Use vague verbs like "understand" or "learn about" — pick a Bloom verb that names a real behavior.
- Number the modules 1–N unless the order actually matters.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you assumed something, call it out — those become my SME questions. If the brief is too thin to design from, ask me one clarifying question first instead of guessing.

pro_tip: If you have actual source material — SOPs, interview transcripts, articles — drop it all into a NotebookLM notebook and run this prompt in there. The outline comes back grounded in your real sources, with citations. Way less hallucinated content, and much easier to defend to an SME.
